Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/sway-git.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-48r_5cwf...

Analyzing sway-git AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-48r_5cwf) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard `source` array with a git repository from the official GitHub URL for the sway project, which aligns with typical AUR packaging practices for `-git` packages. The `b2sums` array correctly sets the checksum for the git source to 'SKIP', which is expected and required for version control sources that are not fixed-content archives. There are no network operations, code downloads, or data exfiltration attempts in the top-level scope of the PKGBUILD or within the `pkgver()` function.

The `pkgver()` function executes locally within the checked-out git repository to calculate a version string based on commit count and hash. This involves `git rev-list` and `git rev-parse`, which are safe when run against the local clone of the official upstream. No command substitution or top-level code performs unauthorized actions. The `_meson_setup` function is defined but not executed during the sourcing phase, and the `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are not run due to the `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` flags.

Thus, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only clone the declared git repository (a transparent operation) and source the PKGBUILD to extract metadata, without executing any potentially dangerous code. All actions are consistent with standard AUR packaging practices and pose no immediate security risk during this phase.
</details>
<summary>
Clones trusted upstream git repo; no malicious execution during sourcing.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Clones trusted upstream git repo; no malicious execution during sourcing.[/]

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable assignments, function definitions, and a `pkgver()` function that runs `meson introspect` and `git rev-list`/`rev-parse` on the cloned local repository. No top-level command substitutions, backticks, or other executable code exist outside of functions. The `source` array references the official upstream git repository (`https://github.com/swaywm/sway`) and two static config files with valid checksums. Even though `prepare()` is not executed (due to `--noprepare`), the `pkgver()` function will attempt to run meson and git commands, but these only read from the local repository and pose no security risk. There are no curl, wget, eval, obfuscated code, or data exfiltration attempts. The use of `SKIP` for the git source’s checksum is expected for VCS packages. This is a legitimate AUR PKGBUILD for `sway-git` with no signs of supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>Legitimate PKGBUILD, no malicious top-level code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Legitimate PKGBUILD, no malicious top-level code.[/]

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/sway-git.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-xiup4z_q...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is straightforward and contains no malicious behavior relevant to sourcing or source fetching.

**Source array**: The only remote fetch is `git+https://github.com/swaywm/sway.git`, which is the legitimate upstream sway repository. The two other sources (`50-systemd-user.conf`, `sway-portals.conf`) are local files expected to reside alongside the PKGBUILD in the AUR repo, with proper b2sums provided.

**Top-level code**: Only a helper function `_meson_setup()` is defined at the top level; it is never invoked at top-level scope, so it executes nothing during sourcing.

**`pkgver()`**: Uses `meson introspect` on a build directory created by `prepare()`, plus standard `git rev-list`/`git rev-parse` commands on the cloned source. With `--noprepare`, the `build-pkgver` directory won't exist and `pkgver()` will likely fail/error, but this is a functional concern, not a security one. No network calls, no command injection, no data exfiltration.

No suspicious patterns (eval, base64, curl|bash, unexpected remote hosts, obfuscated code) are present anywhere in the file.
</details>
<summary>Standard sway-git PKGBUILD; fetches only legitimate upstream sources.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard sway-git PKGBUILD; fetches only legitimate upstream sources.[/]

data.reasoning: Both audits failed at source download stage (makepkg --nobuild), preventing review of critical build/install scripts and source code. PKGBUILD appears safe but full package safety can't be verified without source.
coverage_issues: Critical files not reviewed: PKGBUILD functions (prepare/build/package), .install hooks, sway source code with filesystem/network access
No review of meson.build or other build scripts executed during installation
false_negatives: Potential risks in un-reviewed build scripts (e.g., unsafe command substitutions in meson.build)
Undetected issues in .install hooks or package() function
Source code vulnerabilities (e.g., unsafe setuid usage, path traversal)
re_audit_focus: src/sway source code (particularly main.c and IPC modules)
learnings: Improve error handling for source download failures - retry mechanism or fallback to partial audit
Prioritize PKGBUILD functions over pkgver() during initial review
Include build system files (meson.build) in critical review list
Add network/filesystem access detection in source code as high-priority check
Implement better dependency resolution for VCS packages during audit prep
